title: Chapter 3 (intermediate) — Real-time client/server
meta.desc: Intermediate level: the authoritative server and rooms, the protocol of intents vs snapshots, the shared Phaser-free simulation, hidden information, spectator queue and seat takeover, and return-to-lobby.
hero.title: Real-time client/server
hero.lead: One screen becomes many: a TV, two phones, a referee. We look at the authoritative server, the intents/snapshots protocol, the shared simulation, and the small choreography of seats, spectators and disconnects.
s1.lead: The match is computed in exactly one place: the server. Each game lives in a Room — two player slots, a waiting queue, any number of TV spectators, and one authoritative simulation.
s1.code.c1: // ~30 Hz: both the sim step AND the broadcast rate
s1.code.c3: // queue: spectate until a seat frees
s1.p1: The server (server/index.js) only routes WebSocket messages to the right room: host creates a room, join places a participant in it, and everything else (aim, ready, shell…) is routed to the room's methods. No game logic lives in the network layer.
s1.callout: One source of truth. If each device ran its own match, they'd drift apart (a ball here, not there). The room is the single referee — the screens can only display what it decides.
s2.h2: The protocol: intents up, snapshots down
s2.p1: The two directions of the WebSocket carry very different things. Phones send small intents; the server broadcasts a full game snapshot about 30 times per second.
s2.fig.cap: Phones send tiny orders; the server sends the whole state back to every screen.
s2.p2: Each tick, the room advances the simulation by a fixed step, collects the events that occurred, then broadcasts the snapshot to every subscriber of the room:
s3.h2: The same simulation on every machine
s3.p1: The server runs under Node, with no browser or canvas. It can still compute the physics because all the logic lives in src/sim/, in pure JavaScript. The server imports the same file as the clients:
s3.p2: That's the payoff of chapter 1's architecture decision: the same physics runs on the server (the judge) and stays testable under Node. Nobody rewrites the trajectories “for the network”.
s3.snapomit.c1: // the spectator TV never gets the players' chosen aim values shown;
s3.snapomit.c2: // only ready flags, and — once fired — the resulting projectiles.
s3.callout.nerd: Hidden information. The snapshot does not contain the players' angle or power while aiming — those numbers are never shown on the TV. Only the “ready” flags and, once a shot is fired, the projectiles are broadcast. The information asymmetry is in the protocol.
s4.p1: A room is alive: people arrive, leave, take over. The room handles these transitions without breaking the match. An arriving participant takes a free seat, otherwise they join the queue as a spectator.
s4.code1.cap: server/Room.js — free seat or waiting queue
s4.p2: When a player frees their seat (voluntary leave or disconnect), the front of the queue takes over (promoteInto). And if no one fills both seats mid-match, the room returns to the lobby: the TV shows the code and QR again.
s4.code2.cap: server/Room.js — back to the lobby if a seat stays empty
s4.code2.c1: // a waiting spectator takes the seat
s4.callout: Remember. The network is also all the “unhappy” cases: leaves, reconnects, seats freeing up. Centralising this state in a single Room object makes these transitions readable and testable.
